questions.prompt: Complete: "There are ___ eggs left, just enough for breakfast."
questions.explanation: Eggs is a countable plural, so a small number is a few eggs. A little and much go with uncountable nouns, and any would need a negative or a question, not this affirmative.

questions.prompt: Complete: "Add ___ salt to the soup — not too much."
questions.explanation: Salt is uncountable, so a small amount is a little salt. A few, many and a number of all need countable plural nouns, which salt is not.

questions.prompt: Complete: "How ___ sugar do you take in your coffee?"
questions.explanation: Sugar is uncountable, so we ask How much sugar…?. How many is for countable plurals (How many spoons?), and "how few" / "how lot" are not correct question forms.

questions.prompt: Complete: "I have ___ homework tonight, so I can't go out."
questions.explanation: Homework is uncountable, and a lot of works with both countable and uncountable nouns: a lot of homework. Many, a few and a number of all require countable plurals, which homework is not.

questions.prompt: Choose the sentence with the correct quantifier.
questions.explanation: Time is uncountable, so it pairs with much: We don't have much time. "many time" mismatches (many needs a countable plural), "much friends" mismatches the other way, and "times" wrongly pluralizes the uncountable noun time.

questions.prompt: Complete: "She speaks ___ languages: English, French and Arabic."
questions.explanation: Languages is a countable plural, so several languages fits (it means "more than two"). Much, a little and a great deal of all go with uncountable nouns, so they cannot describe a list of separate languages.
